query: management of bilateral tubal obstruction at cornua following HSG

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Gynecology Infertility Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Clinical Significance -- Importance of tubal Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: Evaluation of tubal patency plays a key role in counseling infertility patients considering IUI or IVF therapies.  Sonohysterosalpingogram (HycoSY) is a quick, simple technique to confirm tubal patency rapidly. A combined SIS with 2D or 3D ultrasound can be performed around day 5 through 9 as a “one-stop-shop” infertility evaluation. [52] HyCoSy is comparable to HSG for assessment of tubal patency when compared with the laparoscopic chromopertubation. [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: An HSG assists clinicians in identifying the patency and contour of the endometrial cavity and fallopian tubes and excludes tubal obstruction or endometrial structural abnormalities as the etiology of female infertility. "Tubal infertility" or "tubal factors" refers to congenital conditions or conditions that cause tubal damage, resulting in female infertility. The most common cause of tubal occlusions is sexually transmitted infections. Neisseria gonorrhoeae is associated with approximately 90% of infertility cases. [4] A recent systematic review demonstrated a positive correlation between >75% of patients with Chlamydia trachomatis infection and female infertility. However, tubal occlusion can also be seen in other conditions, including endometriosis, pelvic adhesions secondary to surgical procedures, or medically managed ectopic pregnancies. False-positive findings for tubal occlusion can result due to tubal spams, which may occur during an HSG procedure in patients with normal anatomic structures. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Evaluation -- Tubal Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The gold standard for the evaluation of tubal patency is laparoscopy with chromopertubation. Laparoscopy is indicated as a first-line diagnostic test for suspected pelvic adhesions, endometriosis, or other pelvic pathologies; however, due to high specificity and being less invasive, the hysterosalpingogram (HSG) is more commonly used for the first-line evaluation for tubal patency and abnormalities. [45] The ability to detect abnormalities is best for proximal occlusion, followed by distal occlusions; however, the HSG has poor predictability for intrauterine and tubal adhesions. [46] Another added benefit of the HSG is increased pregnancy and live birth rates with oil-soluble media. A meta-analysis showed that after HSG, pregnancy, and live birth rates increased compared to controls (overall response, 2.98; 95% CI 1.05-6.37). [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: An HSG is primarily indicated in patients with infertility to assess the patency and contour of the endometrial cavity and fallopian tubes and excludes tubal obstruction or endometrial structural abnormalities as the underlying etiology. Other imaging modalities (eg, pelvic ultrasonography) may be used to detect other pathologies. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Technique or Treatment -- Hysterosalpingogram Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: During the HSG procedure, the following images are obtained at a minimum: Early filling of the uterus with contrast to detect defects After complete filling of the uterus to detect uterine shape Flow of contrast through the fallopian tubes Spill of contrast into the free peritoneal space

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Personnel
retrieved_docs.content: HSG is most commonly performed in a radiology suite by a radiology technician. However, an OB/GYN clinician can also perform the procedure in the office or clinic setting. In most cases, only a single operator is required to perform this procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hysterosalpingogram (HSG) is an imaging procedure in which contrast dye is injected into the uterine cavity, progresses into the fallopian tubes, and ultimately reaches the fimbriated ends adjacent to the ovaries. Fluoroscopy and x-ray images are taken at various stages of the procedure to assess filling, uterine shape, tubal patency, and peritoneal spillage to evaluate the structure of the endometrial cavity and patency of the fallopian tubes. This diagnostic modality is primarily utilized in assessing female infertility. Structural endometrial cavity and fallopian tube abnormalities contribute significantly to infertility, with up to 60% of cases attributed to such issues. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Salpingitis Isthmica Nodosa -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Salpingitis isthmica nodosa, also known as diverticulosis of the fallopian tube, is a condition characterized by nodular swelling of the fallopian tubes, predominantly affecting the isthmus, and is strongly associated with infertility and ectopic pregnancies. The exact cause remains debated but is likely an acquired condition. Diagnosis is often incidental during investigations for infertility or pelvic pain, with hysterosalpingography (HSG) serving as the primary diagnostic tool. Laparoscopy remains the gold standard for direct visualization and assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: Salpingitis Isthmica Nodosa -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: Salpingitis isthmica nodosa is most likely to be identified during an HSG or diagnostic laparoscopy with chromopertubation. If salpingitis isthmica nodosa is identified, referral to a reproductive endocrinologist capable of providing IVF or an appropriately skilled surgeon if fallopian tube reconstruction is desired.

retrieved_docs.title: Sonography Gynecology Infertility Assessment, Protocols, and Interpretation -- Indications -- Saline Infusion Sonogram
retrieved_docs.content: To assess the uterine cavity for pathologies such as polyps, submucosal fibroids, intrauterine synechiae or scarring, and uterine malformations. To assess tubal patency (instead of a traditional hysterosalpingogram (HSG)).

retrieved_docs.title: Salpingitis Isthmica Nodosa -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: HSG is the first-line investigation for infertility and a reliable diagnostic technique for salpingitis isthmica nodosa. [16] [17] Salpingitis isthmica nodosa cannot be excluded unless HSG has been performed within the last 12 months. [18] However, advances in imaging, eg, high-resolution ultrasound and 3-dimensional magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), are improving diagnostic accuracy in some cases. [13] Radiologically, salpingitis isthmica nodosa usually produces periluminar globular collections of contrast (diverticula), typically around the isthmic fallopian tube. [19] These collections are usually grouped together over a 1- to 2-cm section of the tube and are rarely deeper than 2 mm but can form an uninterrupted connection with the tubular lumen. When this occurs, flecks of contrast can be seen above and below the lumen. [20] In some cases, salpingitis isthmica nodosa can lead to hydrosalpinx, tubular occlusion, or involve the uterine cornu interstitium. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Preparation -- Obtaining Informed Consent
retrieved_docs.content: Benefits : Sterilization is a safe, highly effective, hormone-free, permanent form of contraception that is effective immediately and requires no ongoing effort on behalf of the patient. Additionally, the procedure appears to decrease the risk of ovarian cancer. While traditional sterilization methods (eg, clips, bands, and partial salpingectomy) confer some protection, especially against endometrioid and clear cell ovarian carcinomas, bilateral salpingectomy significantly reduces the risk of epithelial ovarian cancer. Epithelial ovarian cancer is responsible for 90% of all ovarian cancers and 90% of all ovarian cancer deaths. [25] [26] A traditional tubal ligation is estimated to reduce ovarian cancer risk by 13% to 41%, while a bilateral salpingectomy reduces the risk by 42% to 78%. [27] Therefore, the American Society of Gynecologic Oncology (SGO) and ACOG recommend that a bilateral salpingectomy be considered in women who desire permanent sterilization to reduce the risk of ovarian cancer. [25] Bilateral salpingectomy also eliminates the chance of a subsequent ectopic tubal pregnancy and appears to have lower failure rates than traditional sterilization methods. [8] Neither traditional sterilization techniques nor bilateral salpingectomy appears to affect ovarian reserve. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: The anatomical structures primarily assessed during an HSG are the fallopian tubes and the endometrial cavity. In patients with normal anatomic reproductive structures, the fallopian tubes are patent and attach to the uterus at the interstitial space. During ovulation, the oocytes released from the ovaries pass through the patent fallopian tube lumens into the endometrial cavity. Each fallopian tube is on either side of the uterus, with the fimbriated end located near the ovary distally and the proximal end attached to the uterine cornua. The fallopian tubes are usually divided into 3 main segments: the infundibulum, ampulla, and isthmus.

retrieved_docs.title: Hysterosalpingogram -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Hysterosalpingogram remains a crucial component in the diagnostic evaluation of female infertility and serves as a complementary imaging modality to other studies, including pelvic ultrasonography, hysteroscopy, magnetic resonance, and laparoscopy with chromotubation. Laparoscopy with chromotubation often helps identify and simultaneously treat tubal and nontubal factors, including intraabdominal and pelvic adhesions and endometriosis. HSG helps evaluate tubal and intrauterine abnormalities (eg, synechiae and polyps). [23] Each modality can provide helpful information for assessing female infertility, but no single study provides a comprehensive assessment. Thus, a combination of transvaginal ultrasonography, HSG, and hysteroscopy is frequently recommended to evaluate patients with infertility. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Asherman Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: It is found in 1.5% of women evaluated with a hysterosalpingogram (HSG) for infertility, between 5 and 39% of women with recurrent miscarriage. Asherman’s Syndrome may occur in 31% of women after the initial hysteroscopic resection of leiomyoma, and up to 46% after the second hysteroscopic resection.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2025
retrieved_docs.content: Endoscopy of ovary and fallopian tube, ligation and crushing of bilateral fallopian tubes, and ligation of bilateral fallopian tubes are interrelated gynecologic procedures that fall under the overarching category of ligation or occlusion of fallopian tubes. Within this hierarchy, ligation and crushing of bilateral fallopian tubes is identified as a specific form of both ligation of bilateral fallopian tubes and the broader ligation or occlusion of fallopian tubes, indicating that it combines tubal ligation with mechanical crushing. Similarly, Endoscopy of ovary and fallopian tube is classified as a subprocedure of ligation or occlusion of fallopian tubes, reflecting its role in the diagnostic or therapeutic evaluation of the tubal structures. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships helps clinicians understand the spectrum of interventions ranging from diagnostic endoscopy to definitive tubal occlusion techniques, facilitating appropriate procedural selection and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1779
retrieved_docs.content: استئصال قناة فالوب (salpingectomy) is categorized under Female Reproductive System Procedures and functions as a parent entity for several specific laparoscopic techniques. The procedures Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, bilateral; Laparoscopic partial salpingectomy, unilateral; Laparoscopic salpingectomy, bilateral; and Laparoscopic salpingectomy, unilateral are all child procedures of استئصال قناة فالوب, representing bilateral or unilateral removal of the fallopian tube, either partially or completely, using minimally invasive laparoscopy. Clinically, استئصال قناة فالوب frequently co‑occurs with مرض التهاب الحوض (pelvic inflammatory disease), reflecting its common use in the management of this infection and its complications. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship of these procedures within female reproductive system interventions and their association with مرض التهاب الحوض helps clinicians select appropriate surgical approaches and anticipate related clinical scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 4 -- Community 2823
retrieved_docs.content: Physicians specializing in Obstetrics & Gynecology are trained to perform salpingo‑oophorectomy procedures, which may involve complete or partial removal of the fallopian tubes and ovaries and can be conducted unilaterally or bilaterally as a separate surgical operation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2070
retrieved_docs.content: The urogenital system is closely linked to specific gynecologic conditions, including ovarian torsion and fallopian tube disorder. Ovarian torsion involves the twisting of the ovary, while fallopian tube disorder refers to abnormalities of the tubal structure. Both conditions are associated with the anatomic sites of the urogenital system, highlighting the importance of evaluating this system when diagnosing and managing ovarian and tubal pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2365
retrieved_docs.content: Ligation and crushing of bilateral fallopian tubes is a specific surgical technique that falls under the broader category of ligation of bilateral fallopian tubes and, more generally, under the umbrella of ligation or occlusion of fallopian tubes. This procedure combines both tying and crushing of the bilateral fallopian tubes to achieve permanent occlusion, representing a particular method within the range of tubal ligation approaches used for permanent contraception.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2361
retrieved_docs.content: Salpingo‑oophorectomy, complete or partial, unilateral or bilateral (separate procedure) is closely related to oophorectomy; unilateral and bilateral, as both surgical interventions involve removal of ovarian tissue and may be performed on one side (unilateral) or both sides (bilateral).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2788
retrieved_docs.content: Salpingectomy (استئصال قناة فالوب) is a surgical procedure that involves the removal of one or both fallopian tubes and is classified within the broader category of Female Reproductive System Procedures. Two common minimally invasive variants are the unilateral laparoscopic partial salpingectomy and the bilateral laparoscopic salpingectomy, both of which are specific subtypes of salpingectomy. Clinically, salpingectomy is associated with an increased risk of abnormal pregnancy (حمل منتبذ), reflecting its impact on tubal function and fertility. The procedure also frequently co‑occurs with pelvic inflammatory disease (مرض التهاب الحوض), indicating that patients with PID may require salpingectomy as part of their management. Understanding these relationships helps clinicians anticipate reproductive outcomes, recognize associated infections, and select the appropriate surgical approach for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: Bisection of bilateral ovaries, supracervical hysterectomy with removal of both fallopian tubes and ovaries, right‑ovary excision (استئصال المبيض الأيمن), drainage of an ovarian cyst by an abdominal approach, endoscopy of the ovary and fallopian tube, and other operations on the ovary are grouped under the procedural family “other operations on ovary,” which has the ovary as its primary anatomical focus. These ovarian surgeries are also considered specific types of endocrine system incisions. Salpingo‑oophorotomy and the broader category “upper female genital tract operation” encompass the same set of ovarian procedures, and ligation or occlusion of the fallopian tubes (including ligation and crushing of bilateral fallopian tubes) is a parent category for endoscopic tubal ligation and for endoscopy of the ovary and fallopian tube. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2411
retrieved_docs.content: Partial removal of the ovary (إزالة المبيض جزئيًا) and partial removal of the uterus (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا) are surgical procedures that frequently co‑occur in clinical practice. Similarly, bilateral removal of the fallopian tubes and ovaries (استئصال البوق والمبيض الثنائي) also commonly co‑occurs with partial hysterectomy (استئصال الرحم جزئيًا). The repeated association of these gynecologic surgeries indicates that, when extensive pelvic pathology or prophylactic indications are present, clinicians often combine ovarian or salpingo‑oophorectomy with a partial hysterectomy to address multiple reproductive organs in a single operative session, thereby streamlining patient care and reducing the need for separate procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1939
retrieved_docs.content: Imaging procedures related to the genital and breast organs encompass a range of techniques, with vaginoscopy (منظار المهبل) identified as a specific sub‑procedure within this category. In the clinical setting of a twin gestation (gestation twin), there is a notable association with the need for other excision of the cervix and uterus, reflecting the potential for cervical or uterine pathology that may arise or be uncovered during a multiple‑pregnancy course. Moreover, the excision of the cervix and uterus frequently co‑occurs with vaginoscopy, indicating that visualization of the vaginal canal and cervical region is often performed in conjunction with surgical removal procedures. This interrelationship underscores the importance of integrating diagnostic imaging (including vaginoscopy) with therapeutic excision techniques when managing complex obstetric cases such as twin pregnancies, thereby facilitating comprehensive assessment and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1532
retrieved_docs.content: Other complications of pregnancy are uterine conditions that arise within the uterus and are clinically associated with optional termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري). This relationship underscores that these complications are localized to the uterine environment and may influence or be influenced by decisions regarding elective pregnancy termination, highlighting the importance of uterine health in the management of pregnancy‑related problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852
retrieved_docs.content: Laparoscopic salpingectomy with removal of tubal pregnancy is a minimally invasive surgical technique that serves as the primary method for removal of ectopic pregnancy, specifically targeting a tubal implantation. This procedure involves the laparoscopic excision of the affected fallopian tube to eliminate the ectopic gestational tissue, thereby preventing complications such as tubal rupture and hemorrhage. By employing a laparoscopic approach, the surgery offers advantages including reduced postoperative pain, shorter hospital stays, and faster recovery compared with traditional open procedures, making it a central component of effective ectopic pregnancy management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with both Ligation of bilateral fallopian tubes and الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ (endometritis). The association with Ligation of bilateral fallopian tubes reflects the possibility of tubal sterilization failure, whereby a pregnancy can occur despite bilateral tubal occlusion. The link with الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ underscores that intrauterine pregnancy may be complicated by inflammation of the uterine lining, which can affect maternal and fetal outcomes. Ligation and crushing of bilateral fallopian tubes is a specific form of Ligation of bilateral fallopian tubes, representing a more detailed surgical technique used to achieve tubal occlusion. Understanding these interconnections is important for clinicians managing contraceptive counseling, recognizing potential pregnancy after sterilization, and addressing infectious complications such as endometritis in pregnant patients.
